summary: Three short tales of troll encounters: a peasant meets a troll at a spring, a girl is lured into a mountain by a handsome troll, and a farmer outwits a troll who steals his harvest. These tales reflect the Scandinavian belief in _bergtagning_ — being taken into the mountain by trolls.
full_text:
TALES OF THE TROLLS
       XIX
moral: Trolls may be powerful, but human wit and faith can overcome them every time.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XVIII: Tales of the Trolls. Communicated from mss. belonging to Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Three folk-tales reflecting primitive faith and superstition; the first is also current in Norway. The later tales tell of women _bergtagen_ (taken into the mountain).

title: The Three Dogs
summary: A king falls under the spell of an evil witch-queen, and only his faithful queen and three magical dogs — with powers of strength, speed, and music — can break the enchantment. From Västergötland, this tale emphasizes the magic of music against trolls.
full_text:
THE THREE DOGS
       XXI
moral: The magic of loyalty and music can break even the strongest evil spell.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XX: The Three Dogs. Source: Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens, p.195 (from Västergötland). Fairy tales hold a high opinion of music's power — the magic flute breaks the evil troll's spell, as in 'Faithful and Unfaithful'.

title: The Poor Devil
summary: When a peasant prays for his cow's protection, God and the Devil argue over who truly controls fate — leading to a wager that reveals the Devil's limitations and God's grace. This tale from Småland is at once humorous and philosophical.
full_text:
THE POOR DEVIL
      XXII
moral: Even the Devil has limits; divine protection is stronger than evil schemes.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXI: The Poor Devil. Source: Bondeson, p.212 (from Småland). A humorous and philosophical tale showing the Devil attempting to rival God.

title: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be
summary: An etiological tale explaining why Småland is rocky and barren while Skåne is flat and fertile — because the Devil tried to create land but was foiled at every turn by God. This humorous origin legend was also used by Selma Lagerlöf in her writings.
full_text:
HOW SMALAND AND SCHONEN CAME TO BE
     XXIII
moral: God's creation is perfect; the Devil's imitations always fall short.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXII: How Smaland and Schonen Came to Be. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. An etiological legend — the Danes and Swedes would say the Devil created these regions, a motif also used by Selma Lagerlöf.

title: The Evil One and Kitta Grau
summary: The Devil meets Kitta Grau, a sharp-tongued old woman, and she out-argues, out-cheats, and out-schemes him at every turn — proving that a clever woman can best even the Prince of Darkness. This tale from Halland shows that a wicked woman outdoes the Devil himself.
full_text:
THE EVIL ONE AND KITTA GRAU
      XXIV
moral: A sharp tongue and quick wit can defeat even the Devil himself.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXIII: The Evil One and Kitta Grau. Source: Bondeson, p.206 (from Halland). Shows that it is child's play for a clever woman to accomplish what the Devil himself cannot.

title: The Lady of Pintorp
summary: A cruel noblewoman makes a pact with the Devil, and after a life of wickedness, her ghost returns to haunt Pintorp Castle in a spectral carriage on stormy nights. The lady may have been a historical figure — the wife of the royal counselor Erik Gyllenstjerna.
full_text:
THE LADY OF PINTORP
       XXV
moral: Evil deeds in life bind the soul beyond death; no wealth can buy redemption from a wicked pact.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXIV: The Lady of Pintorp. Source: Hofberg, p.157. The devil appears in grim Satanic majesty. The evil woman may be a historical figure, the wife of royal counselor Erik Gyllenstjerna.

title: The Rooster, the Hand-Mill and the Swarm of Hornets
summary: A poor peasant trades a pig for three seemingly worthless gifts — a rooster, a hand-mill, and a hornets' nest — but each proves invaluable when he outwits a greedy innkeeper. The gold-laying rooster is a widely known international folktale motif.
full_text:
THE ROOSTER, THE HAND-MILL AND THE SWARM OF HORNETS
     XXVII
moral: The humblest gifts can become the greatest weapons in the hands of the clever.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVI: The Rooster, the Hand-Mill and the Swarm of Hornets. Mss. record by Stephens, from Värmland, communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. A poor peasant receives three splendid gifts. The gold-laying rooster is a widely known motif.

title: Torre Jeppe
summary: A mummified corpse named Torre Jeppe sits in a church night after night until a brave sexton discovers that the ghost cannot rest because of an ancient wrong that must be righted. A ghost-story founded on the belief that wrongdoing torments the guilty even after death.
full_text:
TORRE JEPPE
    XXVIII
moral: Even the dead cannot rest until old wrongs are made right.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVII: Torre Jeppe. Retold and communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund after the mss. version of Hyltén-Cavallius and Stephens. A ghost-story founded on the belief that a wrong torments the doer even after death.

title: The Man Who Died on Holy Innocents' Day
summary: A wicked man named Kalle Kula dies on Holy Innocents' Day and finds himself caught between Heaven and Hell, bargaining for his soul with a blend of humor and desperation. Part fairy-tale, part legend, this tale has a Danish variant and displays the innocently malicious humor of Gottfried Keller.
full_text:
"No sooner had he spoken the words than he was lying in the most
    magnificent room he had ever seen"

    "Then Silverwhite drew his sword with a great sweep, and rushed
    upon the sea-troll"

    "The pike rose to the surface with the golden keys in his mouth"

    "So heartfelt was her happiness that she forgot everything else
    in the world"

    "A shrine adorned with gold and precious stones appeared"

    "The lion turned into a handsome young prince"

    "The rich man had to go along hanging to him like a hawser"

    "He saw a girl sitting in the mountain hall, weaving a web of
    gold"




THE SWEDISH FAIRY BOOK




Swedish Fairy Book




I

KNOeS


Once upon a time there was a poor widow, who found an egg under a pile
of brush as she was gathering kindlings in the forest. She took it and
placed it under a goose, and when the goose had hatched it, a little
boy slipped out of the shell. The widow had him baptized Knoes, and
such a lad was a rarity; for when no more than five years old he was
grown, and taller than the tallest man. And he ate in proportion, for
he would swallow a whole batch of bread at a single sitting, and at
last the poor widow had to go to the commissioners for the relief of
the poor in order to get food for him. But the town authorities said
she must apprentice the boy at a trade, for he was big enough and
strong enough to earn his own keep.

So Knoes was apprenticed to a smith for three years. For his pay he
asked a suit of clothes and a sword each year: a sword of five
hundredweights the first year, one of ten hundredweights the second
year, and one of fifteen hundredweights the third year. But after he
had been in the smithy only a few days, the smith was glad to give him
all three suits and all three swords at once; for he smashed all his
iron and steel to bits.

Knoes received his suits and swords, went to a knight's estate, and
hired himself out as a serving-man. Once he was told to go to the
forest to gather firewood with the rest of the men, but sat at the
table eating long after the others had driven off and when he had at
last satisfied his hunger and was ready to start, he saw the two young
oxen he was to drive waiting for him. But he let them stand and went
into the forest, seized the two largest trees growing there, tore them
out by the roots, took one tree under each arm, and carried them back
to the estate. And he got there long before the rest, for they had to
chop down the trees, saw them up and load them on the carts.

On the following day Knoes had to thresh. First he hunted up the
largest stone he could find, and rolled it around on the grain, so
that all the corn was loosened from the ears. Then he had to separate
the grain from the chaff. So he made a hole in each side of the roof
of the barn, and stood outside the barn and blew, and the chaff and
straw flew out into the yard, and the corn remained lying in a heap on
the floor. His master happened to come along, laid a ladder against
the
moral: Death does not erase a life of wickedness, but humor and desperation may soften even the hardest judgment.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.XXVIII: The Man Who Died on Holy Innocents' Day. Communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. Has a Danish variant. Its innocently malicious humor is worthy of Gottfried Keller.

title: The Changeling
summary: A mother suspects her child has been replaced by a troll changeling and uses clever tricks — including brewing beer in an eggshell — to force the troll mother to return her real baby.
full_text:
In a quiet farming valley in Värmland, a young mother named Kerstin sat by the cradle of her newborn son, little Nils. He had been a healthy, happy baby — until one night when the house was filled with an unnatural wind, the candles flickered blue, and the cattle lowed in terror. The next morning, Nils was different. His eyes, once bright and blue, had turned a muddy gray. His skin had taken on a yellowish pallor. He no longer smiled or cooed. Instead, he stared at the ceiling with a knowing, ancient gaze, and when he cried — which was often — it was a sound like the scraping of stones. He had an enormous appetite but never grew. He would break the furniture, pull the cat's tail until it yowled, and laugh when anyone stumbled.

Kerstin's mother, an old wise woman named Sigrid, came to visit. She looked at the child for a long time, then took Kerstin aside. "That is not your son," she said. "That is a bortbyting — a changeling. The trolls have taken your little Nils into the mountain and left one of their own in his place."

Kerstin wept. "How can I get him back?" she asked.

"You must make the changeling show its true nature," said Sigrid. "Trolls are old, no matter how young they look. They cannot hide their age when faced with something impossible. Listen carefully."

Sigrid instructed Kerstin to brew beer in an eggshell. Not a pot of beer — a single eggshell. She was to set it over the fire and pretend to brew. Then she was to hide and watch.

Kerstin did as she was told. She took an empty eggshell, balanced it over the flames, and pretended to brew beer. Then she crept behind the woodpile and watched.

The changeling sat up in its cradle, its gray eyes narrowing. It watched her for a long time, then climbed out of the cradle, toddled to the fire, and peered at the eggshell. Its face twisted into an expression of ancient contempt.

"I am as old as the forests of Värmland," it said in a voice that was not a child's voice, but the voice of something old and bitter. "I have seen the mountains rise and the seas retreat. I have seen the first king crowned and the last pagan temple burned. And in all my years, I have never — never — seen anyone brew beer in an eggshell."

Kerstin leaped from her hiding place and grabbed a birch rod. "You have admitted it!" she cried. "You are a troll, not my son! If you do not return my little Nils, I will beat you until you howl!"

The changeling shrieked — a sound like a hundred bats taking flight — and vanished in a puff of sulfurous smoke. At that same moment, the front door opened, and in walked a woman tall as a pine tree, with moss in her hair and eyes like dark pools. She carried a bundle wrapped in birch bark. She set it down on the bed. "Take your brat back," she said. "He has been nothing but trouble. Cries all night. Won't eat troll food. Keeps asking for his mother."

She snatched up the changeling and was gone before Kerstin could thank her. Kerstin rushed to the bundle. Inside was little Nils, healthy and whole, his blue eyes blinking in the firelight. He smiled when he saw his mother and reached out his tiny arms.

From that day on, Kerstin always placed a piece of steel over the cradle at night and kept a bowl of porridge with butter on the doorstep for any passing troll who might be hungry. And little Nils grew up strong and clever, though his mother never told him the whole story.

Sometimes, when the wind howls around the cottage and the fire casts long shadows, the old women of Värmland still warn young mothers: "Never leave your child alone at night, and never trust a silent baby. For the bortbyting is patient, and the trolls are always watching."
moral: A mother's love and quick thinking can overcome even the trickery of trolls.
source: Swedish folklore (Selma Lagerlöf)

title: The Boy Who Ate with the Giant
summary: A clever little boy uses a series of tricks — a bag of porridge, a handful of bird feathers, and a stone — to convince a giant that he is stronger and hungrier than the giant himself.
full_text:
In the province of Småland, where the forests stretch dark and deep between the rocky fields, there once lived a poor farmer and his wife. They had many children, and the youngest was a boy so small that they called him Lill-Petter. But what Lill-Petter lacked in size, he made up for in cleverness.

One autumn, when the crops had failed and the family had nothing to eat, Lill-Petter said to his father, "Let me go into the forest. Perhaps I can find us a deer or a hare."

His father laughed. "You? You are so small a hare would mistake you for a tuft of grass! Stay home and help your mother."

But Lill-Petter did not stay home. He took a small bag, filled it with porridge, slung it over his shoulder, and walked into the dark forest.

He walked until the sun was high, then until it began to sink. And at last, he came to a clearing where a giant sat on a boulder the size of a cottage. The giant was eating a whole roasted boar, bones and all. He crunched the ribs like twigs and swallowed the hooves whole.

The giant noticed Lill-Petter and laughed — a sound like boulders rolling down a mountain. "Well, well! A little morsel! Have you come to be my dinner?"

"Not at all," said Lill-Petter, bowing politely. "I have come to challenge you."

"Challenge me?" The giant's laughter shook the leaves from the trees. "To what?"

"To an eating contest," said Lill-Petter. "Whoever can eat more shall be the winner."

The giant laughed even harder. "Little boy, I could eat a hundred of you and still be hungry! But I accept your challenge. What are the stakes?"

"If I win," said Lill-Petter, "you must give me your treasure — all the gold and silver you have gathered."

"And if I win?" asked the giant.

"Then you may eat me," said Lill-Petter with a smile.

They sat down across from each other. The giant produced a cauldron of porridge the size of a washtub. "We will take turns," said the giant. "I will go first."

He plunged his face into the cauldron and ate and ate until the porridge was half gone. Steam rose from his ears. His belly swelled.

"Your turn," he said, wiping his mouth with his sleeve.

Lill-Petter stepped forward. He had secretly cut a hole in the bottom of his bag, through which the porridge leaked onto the ground. He held the bag to his mouth and pretended to eat, while the porridge drained away. "Delicious!" he said. "But this is child's play. I am barely warmed up!"

The giant stared in disbelief as Lill-Petter "ate" the entire remaining half of the porridge.

"Now," said Lill-Petter, "let us have a squeezing contest!" He picked up a round stone from the ground and squeezed it until a few drops of water from a nearby puddle ran down his wrist. "See? I squeezed water from this stone! Let me see you do better."

The giant grabbed a massive boulder and squeezed with all his might. But of course, no water came from the dry rock. "Impossible!" roared the giant. "You cheated!"

"I did not," said Lill-Petter. "You are simply too weak. But I will give you one more chance. Let us have a throwing contest."

Lill-Petter reached into his pocket and pulled out a handful of bird feathers he had gathered. He threw them into the air, and they scattered across the sky. "There! I threw these feathers so high that they touched the clouds!"

The giant picked up a stone and hurled it with all his strength. It flew over the trees and disappeared. "I threw that one higher!" he insisted.

"Ah, but did it touch the clouds?" asked Lill-Petter. "No. Mine did. I win."

The giant hung his enormous head. He had never been beaten by anyone, let alone a tiny boy. "You are right," he said. "You are stronger and cleverer than I. My treasure is yours."

He led Lill-Petter into his cave, where piles of gold and silver coins glittered in the torchlight. Lill-Petter filled his bag as much as he could carry.

"Wait," said the giant. "I have one more thing to give you." He reached into a corner and pulled out a small wooden box. "This is a magic flute. When you play it, anyone who hears must dance. It may serve you well."

Lill-Petter thanked the giant and returned home. His family was overjoyed to see him, and when he emptied his bag of gold onto the table, they could scarcely believe their eyes. They never went hungry again.

And ever after, when anyone in Småland tells the story of Lill-Petter, they call him "Pojken som åt i kapp med jätten" — the boy who ate in a race with the giant — and they remember that cleverness is stronger than strength.

As for the giant, some say he learned humility that day. Others say he moved deeper into the forest, where he still tells the tale of the tiny boy who out-ate, out-squeezed, and out-threw him.
moral: Cleverness and wit can overcome even the greatest strength.
source: Swedish folklore
